elements.sent-2411-001: However, agents trained in static environments lack generalization capabilities, limiting their potential in broader scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: Thus, recent benchmarks frequently rely on multiple environments, for instance, by providing stochastic noise, simple permutations, or altogether different settings.
elements.sent-2411-003: In practice, such collections result mainly from costly human-designed processes or the liberal use of random number generators.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we introduce AMaze, a novel benchmark generator in which embodied agents must navigate a maze by interpreting visual signs of arbitrary complexities and deceptiveness.
elements.sent-2411-005: This generator promotes human interaction through the easy generation of feature-specific mazes and an intuitive understanding of the resulting agents' strategies.
elements.sent-2411-006: As a proof-of-concept, we demonstrate the capabilities of the generator in a simple, fully discrete case with limited deceptiveness.
elements.sent-2411-007: Agents were trained under three different regimes (one-shot, scaffolding, interactive), and the results showed that the latter two cases outperform direct training in terms of generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-008: Indeed, depending on the combination of generalization metric, training regime, and algorithm, the median gain ranged from 50\% to 100\% and maximal performance was achieved through interactive training, thereby demonstrating the benefits of a controllable human-in-the-loop benchmark generator.
elements.sent-2411-009: Benchmark, Human-in-the-loop, Generalization, Mazes, Reinforcement Learning \end{abstract \end{frontmatter.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Based on the need to fairly compare algorithms \citep{Islam2018 , benchmarks have proliferated in the Reinforcement Learning (RL) community.}
elements.sent-2411-011: These cover a wide range of tasks, from the full collection of Atari 2600 games \citep{Bellemare2013 to 3D simulations in Mujoco \citep{Laskin2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, in recent years, the focus of research has changed from producing more complex environments to producing a range of environments.
elements.sent-2411-013: Although undeniable progress has been made with respect to the capabilities of trained agents, much remains to be done for their capacity to generalize \citep{Mnih2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In practice, agents ``will not learn a general policy, but instead a policy that will only work for a particular version of a particular task with particular initial parameters''~\citep{Risi2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, a recurring theme in modern RL research is the training of agents in various situations to avoid overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-016: Although some algorithms have built-in solutions to smooth out the learning process, e.g. TD3 \citep{Fujimoto2018 (where small perturbations are applied to the actions), providing such a diversity of experience primarily originates from the environments themselves.}
elements.sent-2411-017: To this end, numerous benchmarks now consist of a collection with varying degrees of homogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-018: Some of them have a similar structure, as in the Sonic benchmark \citep{Nichol2018 where levels are small areas taken from three games in the franchise.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In other cases, environments share very little: In the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE), the single common factors are the dimensions of the observation space \citep{Bellemare2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Intermediate test suites with distinct but complementary sets of ``skill-building'' tasks have also been designed, for example, with the Mujoco simulator \citep{Wawrzynski2009, Yu2019, Laskin2021 or Meta-World \cite{Yu2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, all of these examples share a common feature: the set of environments is predefined, generally the result of a costly human-tailored design procedure, e.g. \citep{Beattie2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: To solve this generalization problem, agents must face sufficiently diverse situations so that the underlying principles are learned instead of a specific trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-023: Naturally, this requires generating environments that exhibit such diversity while still offering the same core challenges.
elements.sent-2411-024: One common way to address this later point is to use procedural generation \citep{Beattie2016,Kempka2016,Harries2019,Juliani2019,Tomilin2022 or complementary techniques such as evolutionary algorithms \citep{Alaguna2018,Wang2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, ProcGen \citep{Cobbe2020 encompasses 16 different types of environment and serves as a generalizable alternative to ALE.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Adapting more recent video game environments, either directly \citep{Synnaeve2016 or in a light format \citep{Tian2017 , can help further push adaptability by providing finer-grained perceptions and actions.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: While such an approach can be used to create large training sets, the main difficulty becomes the design of a sufficiently tunable generator, i.e. one in which desirable features are easy to introduce.
elements.sent-2411-028: Considering the challenges of generating a panel of demanding training environments, the contribution of this article is two-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-029: We introduce AMaze\footnote{AMaze library is available on PyPI at \url{https://pypi.org/project/amaze-benchmarker/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The code for the experiment described thereafter is hosted at \url{https://github.com/kgd-al/amaze_edhucat_2024 , a generator for generic, computationally inexpensive environments of unbounded complexity that focus on generalization (via environmental diversity) and intelligibility (intuitive human understanding).}
elements.sent-2411-031: We demonstrate how such a generator is helpful in leading to more generalized performance (robust behavior w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-032: After highlighting, in \autoref{sec:litterature , the niche this generator occupies in the current benchmark literature, we describe its main components in \autoref{sec:mazes .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Three alternative methodologies for training generalized maze-navigating agents are then detailed in \autoref{sec:training alongside two algorithms (A2C and PPO).}
elements.sent-2411-034: The resulting performance in handling unknown environments is then thoroughly tested in \autoref{sec:eval , allowing us to draw conclusions about the relative benefits of the generator, the training processes, and the underlying algorithms.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{ Comparison between AMaze and related benchmark (suites). All time metrics correspond to the wall time for 1000 timesteps of the corresponding environment, averaged over 10 replicates. Qualification of the inputs, outputs, and control levels are taken from the related article or, when unavailable, directly from the sources. Overall, AMaze is competitive with small-scale benchmarks, but provides the experimenter with more control over the characteristics of the targeted environments. Complex environments (e.g. 3D) have much higher computational costs making AMaze an efficient and scalable prototyping platform. } \label{tab:benchmarks} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{benchmarking}
elements.sent-2411-035: As our library is primarily targeted at Python environments, we restricted the set of considered environments to those that could be reliably installed and used on an experimenter's machine.
elements.sent-2411-036: This led to the exclusion of the Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning Benchmark \citep{Laskin2021 and RetroGym \citep{Nichol2018 due to missing dependencies or tools.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: On similar ground, Obstacle Tower \citep{Juliani2019 could not be evaluated here, despite the extensive control it gives the experimenter, as it required an independent Unity server to run, making it impractically slow.}
elements.sent-2411-038: As detailed in the following section, AMaze can provide environments for fully discrete, fully continuous, and hybrid agents.
elements.sent-2411-039: Based on the time taken to simulate 1000 timesteps, only the simplest of the gymnasium suite \citep{Sutton2018 is comparable to AMaze which, in addition, provides numerous unique and experimenter-controlled environments.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In the hybrid case, where agents perceive images but still only take discrete steps, the library is on par with Classic Control tasks \citep{Barto1983 such as Mountain Car or Cart Pole.}
elements.sent-2411-041: ProcGen \citep{Cobbe2020 addresses similar concerns as AMaze and is quite comparable in terms of speed, but has a stronger focus on randomness, with difficulty levels being the main way of controlling the resulting environments.}
elements.sent-2411-042: DeepMind Lab2D \citep{Beattie2020 , while noticably slower, is also extensively customizable, albeit through lua scripting, and allows for heterogeneous multi-agent experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-043: With respect to the fully discrete case, the most computationally expensive of the three regimes, AMaze performs at a level similar to that of Box2D or Mujoco \citep{Todorov2012 , which, in traditional implementations such as gymnasium \citep{Towers2024 , lack customization capabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Purely vision-based benchmarks such as ALE \citep{Bellemare2013 , Meta-world \citep{Yu2019 , LevDoom \citep{Tomilin2022 or Maze Explorer \citep{Harries2019 , while offering a more challenging task than AMaze, also exhibit drastically higher costs with variable levels of experimenter control over the environments.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: It follows that AMaze fills a very specific niche in the benchmarking landscape by providing a computationally inexpensive framework to design challenging environments.
elements.sent-2411-046: Control over the various characteristics of said environments is left in the hands of the experimenter through a number of high- and low-level parameters that will be described in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-047: Learning to navigate mazes represents a flexible, diverse, yet challenging testbed for training agents.
